note:
## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Diane is a 28-year-old female with a past medical history of depression and hypertension who presents for emergency room follow-up after an episode of very high blood pressure. [4][5][7]  
Yesterday, while out for a walk, she felt light-headed and started to fall but was caught by her boyfriend, after which they went to the ER. [6]  
In the ER, her blood pressure was reportedly very high (almost 200) and she also had a headache. [7][10]  
She reports her blood pressures have been running high in the past, with episodes where it “skyrockets” about once a week to once a month. [11][12]  
She notes these spikes often occur during weeks when she is traveling a lot for work and not eating as well, and she does not bring her blood pressure cuff when traveling. [14][18]  
She confirms she did purchase a blood pressure cuff previously and is currently taking lisinopril. [19][20][21][22]  
For depression, she started therapy last year and has been going weekly with good benefit, and she reports a strong support system (boyfriend, mom, brother). [25][26][28]  
Currently, she reports a little nasal congestion attributed to seasonal allergies and denies other symptoms such as chest pain or shortness of breath. [29][30]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Blood pressure in the office today is still mildly elevated. [34]  
Neck exam: no carotid bruits appreciated. [36]  
Cardiac exam: slight 2/6 systolic ejection murmur. [36]  
Lungs: clear to auscultation bilaterally. [36]  
Extremities: trace pitting edema of the lower extremities bilaterally. [36]  

## RESULTS
ECG reviewed and appears unchanged from last year, with changes attributed to chronic high blood pressure. [41]  
Echocardiogram from a few months ago shows a slightly leaky heart valve, and the murmur is stable with no intervention needed. [43]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
**1) Hypertension, uncontrolled with episodic severe elevations (ER BP ~200) and office BP still elevated:** Increase lisinopril to 40 mg once daily. [7][34][45]  
She will continue home blood pressure monitoring and report readings via the patient portal; medication adjustments and possibly adding a second agent will be considered if needed. [45]  

**2) Depression, stable on therapy:** No medication changes; continue weekly therapy, and patient may contact clinician if she needs anything. [26][47]

note:
## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Alan is a 61-year-old male who presents with shortness of breath. [4]  
Over the weekend, after a long bike ride (~3 hours), he became lightheaded and short of breath, which was unusual for him because he bikes every morning without prior issues. [6]  
He denied chest pain initially but reported he may have had a little chest pain after the episode that resolved after about an hour. [9][10]  
He denied fever and reported no sick contacts at home. [7][12]  
He reported a mild cough with a recent cold (first in ~1.5 years) that resolved after about a week. [14]  
He denied nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. [17][18]  
His migraines have been under control with medication and avoiding bright lights, and he is still taking Imitrex. [20][22]  
His reflux has been well controlled on Protonix. [23][26]  
He had cataract surgery about 4.5 months ago and reports his vision has been great since then. [27][28]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Vital signs were reviewed and described as quite good, with perfect oxygenation. [31][32]  
Neck was supple without jugular venous distension. [34]  
Cardiac exam showed regular rhythm without murmur. [34]  
Lung exam revealed slight bilateral expiratory wheeze. [34]  
Trace lower extremity edema was noted. [34]  

## RESULTS
Chest X-ray was normal with no pulmonary edema and no pneumonia. [36]  
Pulmonary function tests were normal. [39]  
Laboratory results were reviewed and described as normal/“quite good.” [39]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
Exertional shortness of breath/lightheadedness with wheeze: clinician impression is possible exercise- and allergy-related asthma exacerbation. [39]  
Start albuterol inhaler: 2 puffs every 4 hours as needed for wheezing or shortness of breath. [41][43]  
Pulmonary referral ordered. [41][43]  
GERD: stable; continue Protonix. [44][26]  
Migraines: stable; continue Imitrex as needed. [44][45]  
Follow up in a couple of weeks, and patient instructed to call right away if shortness of breath recurs. [46][47]

note:
## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Joyce is a 50-year-old female with a history of COPD and congestive heart failure who presents for follow-up of an abnormal lab finding (low hemoglobin). [4]  
The clinician reviewed her labs and noted a low hemoglobin, prompting this follow-up visit. [4]  
Over the past couple of months, she has felt increasingly tired with decreased energy, worsening over the past month and limiting her ability to keep up with her usual running/climbing exercise routine. [5]  
She denies blood in the stool, dark/black stools, and heavy menstrual spotting. [6][7][8][9][10][11]  
She denies chest pain, shortness of breath, fever, chills, and body aches, and reports only mild, non-significant weight loss that she attributes to exercise. [12][13][14][15]  
Regarding COPD, she reports she quit smoking cold turkey and has not resumed, and notes breathing can be a bit harder with seasonal changes which she attributes to allergies. [16][17]  
Regarding CHF, she reports good adherence to a low-salt diet and denies leg swelling. [18][19][23][24]  
She reports a good support system including her boyfriend and brother who lives nearby. [25][26]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Vital signs in the office were reported as “look good.” [29]  
Neck exam: no cervical lymphadenopathy. [31]  
Cardiac exam: slight 2/6 systolic ejection murmur (previously heard). [31]  
Lung exam: clear to auscultation. [31]  
Abdominal exam: mild right lower quadrant tenderness to palpation. [31]  
Extremities: no lower extremity edema. [31]  

## RESULTS
Hemoglobin was 8.2 (noted to be low compared with an expected ~13–14). [35]  
Anemia lab workup was reviewed; some results were still pending at the time of the visit. [37]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
**1) Anemia / low hemoglobin (Hgb 8.2) with fatigue and dizziness**: Plan to await pending anemia labs to determine anemia type and next steps. [12][35][37][39]  
Given concern for possible occult GI blood loss despite no reported melena/hematochezia, plan to schedule endoscopy and colonoscopy. [6][8][39]  
Clinician will stay in close contact over the next week or so and follow up when labs return. [39][44]  
Referral to gastroenterology was ordered for endoscopy/colonoscopy. [42][44]  

**2) COPD (stable, former smoker)**: No further referrals felt necessary at this time; continue to avoid smoking, and no inhalers felt needed currently. [16][17][41]  

**3) Congestive heart failure**: Continue current medications including Toprol, lisinopril, and Lasix 20 mg daily. [41]

note:
## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ashley James is a 14-year-old previously healthy female presenting for evaluation of an abnormal renal ultrasound showing an atrophic/smaller right kidney. [0]  
Per family, she developed acute right-sided abdominal pain “out of nowhere,” severe enough that she was bent over, and the family initially worried about appendicitis. [7][9]  
She was initially treated as a stomach virus with fluids and sent home, but symptoms worsened the next day. [11]  
Further testing (including ultrasound per family) suggested one kidney was larger, and subsequent evaluation in Jacksonville reportedly concluded one kidney was not working well and was “atrophic”/much smaller than the other. [13][17]  
During that evaluation, the family was told there were “cysts or something” on the kidney and ovaries, swollen lymph nodes, and an infection. [19]  
She later came to Atlanta for about four days where she was treated and improved, with no further vomiting, though she continues to have abdominal pain. [21][23]  
She continues to report stomach pain with a burning sensation, especially with eating. [25][57]  
She reports intermittent urinary hesitancy (sometimes hard to start urinating). [59]  
She denies hematuria. [60][61]  
Family reports intermittent burning with urination when she drinks certain beverages (e.g., soda/carbonation), so she mainly drinks Gatorade or water. [63][64]  
Past history includes multiple hospitalizations at age 4 (3–4 times, sometimes for 3 days) for recurrent febrile illness treated with fluids and antibiotics, with episodes recurring over a couple months and then resolving. [29][31]  
Current medications include Pepcid 40 mg daily and Claritin for allergies. [33][35]  
There were no reported pregnancy or delivery complications. [36][37]  
Family history is notable for renal disease on the maternal side (mother’s father, aunt, and cousins) and maternal grandmother with breast cancer. [37][39]  
Family history details include a relative with obstructive uropathy/urinary obstruction leading to kidney transplant and death in his 40s, with clarification that he was on dialysis at the time of death (not necessarily the cause). [40][42][45][92][93][94]  
She has previously seen GI and genetics, and the genetic panel reportedly did not show clinically significant mutations. [54][55]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
General: Well-appearing female, alert and oriented, sitting comfortably, no acute distress. [70]  
Cardiopulmonary: Lungs and heart “sound great.” [69][70]  
Remainder of exam: Within normal limits per clinician. [70]  

## RESULTS
Clinician reviewed that kidney function and blood pressure “look good.” [70]  
Assessment of imaging/history indicates the right kidney is smaller, with the left kidney likely working harder and appearing larger. [72]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
Abnormal renal ultrasound with smaller/atrophic right kidney and compensatory larger left kidney: No intervention needed today; continue monitoring. [0][72]  
Monitoring plan: Annual renal ultrasound to monitor kidney size/overall function, annual urinalysis to ensure no protein or blood in urine, and annual blood pressure check. [74]  
Risk counseling: Discussed higher risk for future hypertension and proteinuria and the need to keep an eye on kidney function and blood pressure. [70]  
Medication counseling: Avoid NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) and use acetaminophen (Tylenol) instead. [74]  
Lifestyle counseling: Encourage hydration (especially with sports) and avoid dehydration; recommended at least 2 liters of water per day. [76][77][78]  
Health maintenance: Recommended healthy diet and continuing an active lifestyle. [80]  
Follow-up: Next appointment can be with primary care or an adult nephrologist closer to home; advised to call if symptoms worsen or questions arise. [82][84]

## RESULTS
Spine x-ray showed no fractures. [48] X-ray demonstrated the prior surgery site and appeared healed properly. [48]

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
1) **Low back pain (suspected muscle pull related to spinal misalignment; no current radicular symptoms).** [14][48]

note:
## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Emily presents with sudden visual distortions and blurring of vision. [4][5]  
She reports decreased vision when reading and that straight lines/door frames appear crooked. [10][11]  
She also reports difficulty adjusting to dim lights when out at a restaurant with her husband. [13][15]  
She currently smokes about one pack of cigarettes per day and has had difficulty fully quitting. [22][23]  
Regarding blood pressure, she checks it when she can remember, and the clinician emphasized regular checks and keeping a log. [24][25][26]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Anterior segment exam: posterior chamber intraocular lenses (PCIOL) in both eyes. [26]  
Right eye: cornea clear, no MVI, cup-to-disc 0.2; fundus with retina attached 360° without tears/holes; posterior segment with drusen in the macula, retinal thickening, subretinal fluid, exudates, and hemorrhage. [26]  
Left eye: cornea clear, no MVI, cup-to-disc 0.3; no hemorrhage or subretinal fluid; retina attached. [26]  

## RESULTS
OCT shows drusen deposits at the retinal pigment epithelium with subretinal fluid accumulation and a visible choroidal neovascular membrane. [26]  
Fluorescein angiography shows focal areas of leakage in the right eye consistent with neovascular age-related macular degeneration. [26]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
Neovascular (wet) age-related macular degeneration, right eye, based on exam and diagnostic testing. [26]  
The clinician discussed that this is an advanced stage that can lead to severe vision loss due to abnormal blood vessels growing beneath the retina and leaking fluid/blood, potentially causing a central blind spot. [26]  
Plan: start intravitreal Lucentis injections once monthly (after numbing the eye), with ongoing regular injections over time to slow vision decline or prevent further loss, though progression may still occur despite treatment. [26]  
Patient had no questions and agreed with the plan. [27]

note:
## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Jaqueline presents after injuring her right ankle during a mountain biking lesson when she fell off her bike and twisted the ankle outward. [0][5][6][7][9][11]  
She reports pain primarily over the outer (lateral) ankle. [12][13]  
She reports some numbness but no tingling in the toes/other areas, and also mentions “numbing” in her heart. [14][15][17]  
She can move the ankle only a little bit and is not really able to walk on it, with limping. [19][20][21][24][25]  
She tried Tylenol without relief and reports hearing a “crack/crackle” at the time of injury. [26][27][31][32][33]  
When asked about fever/chills, she stated she had a “fever” but described it as “like two hundred,” and the clinician noted she was not febrile in clinic. [48][49][50][51][52]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Vital signs in clinic: T 98.6°F, BP 118/77, HR 77 bpm. [52]  
Right ankle: edema and erythema over the lateral malleolus with associated ecchymosis and a few excoriations, with pain to palpation. [52][53][54]  
Right ankle range of motion is decreased in flexion/extension and internal/external rotation. [54]  
No apparent injury to the knee or foot on exam. [54]  

## RESULTS
Right ankle x-ray showed no acute fracture or bony abnormality, consistent with a soft tissue injury. [54]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
Acute right ankle sprain (right), with negative x-ray for fracture. [54][56]  
Place in an air cast and provide crutches. [56]  
Start meloxicam 15 mg once daily. [56]  
Ice the ankle for ~20 minutes five times per day and weight-bear as tolerated. [56]  
Expected improvement over the next couple of weeks; if not improving, return for reassessment and consider physical therapy and/or further imaging. [56]

## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Betty presents for follow-up after a recent heart attack treated with stent placement in the right coronary artery. [0]  
She reports feeling thankful to be alive but is still struggling and describes the event as scary. [1]  
She was hospitalized for about 48 hours and has been home for a couple of weeks. [2]  
Since discharge, she has been taking it easy, doing only very light activity, and taking her medications; she feels improved compared with before but not back to normal. [3]  
She denies recurrent chest pain since the stent was placed, with only mild post-procedure soreness that has improved. [4][5]  
Her wrist access site was red for a while but is healing, and she does not think there is an infection. [6][7]  
She reports shortness of breath when going up and down stairs. [10][11]  
She is taking Brilinta twice daily (every 12 hours) and baby aspirin once daily at night, and she reports no cost issues with Brilinta. [12][13][15][16][17]  
She is working on diet changes, eating well during the week with occasional pizza on weekends, and has been eating vegetables and chicken without added salt. [20][21][23]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Vital signs: BP 127/60 in office. [28]  
Neck: No jugular venous distention and no carotid bruits. [28]  
Cardiac: 3/6 systolic ejection murmur heard at the left base. [28]  
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. [28]  
Extremities/MSK: Right radial artery cardiac cath site clean/dry/intact with no hematoma and palpable radial pulse. [28]  

## RESULTS
Hospital echocardiogram showed ejection fraction about 50% (low-normal). [22]  
Echocardiogram also showed mild mitral regurgitation, felt to explain the murmur. [28]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
**1) Inferior myocardial infarction, s/p right coronary artery stent (recent), with exertional dyspnea on stairs** [0][11][28]  
- Continue dual antiplatelet therapy: aspirin daily lifelong (until instructed otherwise) and Brilinta for at least 1 year to prevent stent closure/recurrent MI. [18][28]  
- Continue Crestor 40 mg once daily. [28]  
- Continue Toprol 50 mg once daily. [34]  
- Order repeat echocardiogram in ~6 months. [28]  
- Enroll in cardiac rehabilitation for graded return to exercise and dietary education/counseling. [32]  
- Follow up in ~6 weeks. [34]  

**2) Post–cardiac catheterization radial access site, healing** [6][7][28]  
- No specific intervention noted today given clean/dry/intact site and palpable pulse. [28]  

**3) Hyperlipidemia / secondary prevention monitoring** [28][34]  
- Order lipid panel and liver function studies for next visit. [34]  

**Activity guidance** [31][32]  
- May resume swimming now given ~2 weeks out and cath site looks good; advised to start with a few laps at a time. [32]

## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
The patient presents with right knee pain that began after falling off a tandem bike with their partner and hitting the knee on a curb about 2 weeks ago. [7][8][10][11]  
They report the knee was normal prior to this injury. [16][17]  
Since the fall, the knee has been sore and “kind of hurting every day,” with symptoms lingering rather than improving. [11][13]  
Pain is worse on the inside/medial aspect and is tender to touch. [33][35][37]  
They can fully straighten the knee but note it sometimes hurts a little and “almost like it clicks.” [43][44]  
They report the knee sometimes feels unstable/shifts when walking, and walking up hills/inclines is particularly painful. [56][57][59]  
They tried ibuprofen, which helps temporarily but the pain returns; they were concerned about taking it longer than 2 weeks per the label. [11][13]  
They have not been swimming since the injury and usually go on walks with their dog for exercise. [21][77]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Right knee: medial joint line tenderness. [33][37][38]  
Pes anserine bursa, epicondyle, popliteal space, and lateral knee were nontender. [40][42][43]  
Skin: dime-sized dry abrasion over the medial patella, appearing about a week old; no scars noted. [43]  
Range of motion: full extension and full symmetric flexion bilaterally. [44][45][50]  
Stability testing: MCL and LCL intact; mild laxity with posterior drawer (~1+); anterior drawer and Lachman negative. [51][52]  
McMurray test negative. [54][56]  
Patella tracks well with no crepitus. [54]  

## RESULTS
Right knee X-rays obtained today appeared normal/healthy with no obvious fracture or bony abnormality; patella tracking and joint space/cartilage appearance were described as normal. [84][85][90]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
Right knee injury after bicycle fall with exam notable for 1+ posterior drawer and subjective instability, concerning for posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) sprain/injury. [7][11][52][57][90]  
Obtain MRI of the right knee to evaluate for PCL injury and assess for other internal derangements (e.g., meniscus or cartilage injury). [92][95]  
Initial management: knee brace fitting today (non-custom/temporary) and referral to physical therapy to restore motion and strength, with plan to reassess in 4–6 weeks. [92][110][112][113]  
Pain/swelling management: ice packs a few times per day and ibuprofen up to three times daily for a short period if helpful; clinician will review dosing. [98][100]  
Medication option: trial a prescription anti-inflammatory taken twice daily (with breakfast and dinner), with option to return to ibuprofen if it works better; prescription to be called into pharmacy. [102][104][106][108]  
Surgery is unlikely for an isolated PCL injury but may be considered if MRI shows additional injury or if instability persists after a period of bracing/therapy. [92][95]

Betty presents with left shoulder pain for the past few weeks. [1]

## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Matthew presents with acute right knee pain after playing basketball yesterday (first time in two years). [0][1][3] He reports that when he came down from a rebound he felt his knee “pop,” followed by swelling and difficulty walking at the time. [1] Pain is located on the inside/medial aspect of the right knee and is described as achy. [8][9][13] Swelling has improved somewhat overnight, and current pain is 4/10. [15][23] He can bear weight now but is favoring the leg, and resting/offloading helps more than anything. [11][21] He tried Tylenol yesterday with a little relief. [17][19]  

For hypertension follow-up, he reports taking amlodipine (Norvasc) 5 mg daily. [24][25] He does not check BP often, but reports readings typically in the 140s–150s systolic with diastolic around 80 (e.g., ~140/80). [27][29]  

For diabetes follow-up, his A1c was 7.2. [34] He is prescribed metformin 500 mg daily but sometimes skips doses due to stomach upset. [34][35] He checks blood sugars only occasionally, and when he does they are around 150. [37][39]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Lungs: “sound good.” [40]  
Cardiac: grade 2/6 systolic ejection murmur, longstanding and unchanged per clinician. [40]  
Right knee: pain to palpation over the medial aspect with edema/swelling, ecchymosis, and some redness; pain with bending/straightening; varus/valgus testing negative. [40][41][42][44]  

## RESULTS
Right knee x-ray was normal with no fractures or bony abnormalities. [44]  
Hemoglobin A1c: 7.2. [34]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
1) **Right knee injury—suspected MCL strain vs tear** based on medial knee tenderness/swelling and normal x-ray; MRI needed to determine tear. [44] Continue Tylenol for pain. [44] Start meloxicam 15 mg for pain and swelling. [44] Place in a knee immobilizer and have patient stay off the knee for the next couple of weeks. [44] Order MRI of the right knee. [44]  

2) **Hypertension—above goal** (home readings often 140s–150s/80; goal discussed ~120/80). [29][30] Increase/adjust Norvasc from current 5 mg due to elevated BP. [24][46]  

3) **Type 2 diabetes—suboptimally controlled** (A1c 7.2; inconsistent metformin use; infrequent glucose checks). [34][35][37] Begin daily home blood glucose monitoring with a log book to guide medication adjustments. [46] Refer to nutrition to improve diet. [46] Counsel to avoid excess strawberries due to sugar content given diabetes control. [49][50]  

4) **Follow-up**: return visit in 2 weeks. [46][54]

note:
## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Thompson is a 67-year-old man with a history of essential hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and osteoarthritis who presents for evaluation of elevated and fluctuating blood pressures. [0]  
Over the last 3–4 weeks, he has had intermittent lightheadedness and slight headache, and home blood pressure checks have occasionally shown systolic readings >180. [3]  
He reports that when he checks his blood pressure during lightheaded episodes, it has still been on the high side. [5]  
He denies recent syncope. [6][7]  
He reports occasional mild chest heaviness but no sustained chest pain and no shortness of breath. [9]  
He notes mild unsteadiness during dizzy episodes and describes the dizziness as lightheaded/fuzzy rather than vertigo. [11][13]  
He denies fever, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. [14][15]  
He denies palpitations/heart racing. [16][17]  
He is taking lisinopril 20 mg twice daily and reports missing doses very rarely, using a pillbox and (despite some confusion) an app to stay on schedule. [18][19][21][23]  
He is also taking atorvastatin for hyperlipidemia. [26][27]  
His osteoarthritis is generally controlled; he remains active with golf but sometimes plays less or skips a day when the weather changes, and he uses Tylenol and activity modification for mild pain. [29][31]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Overall exam was described as normal/unremarkable aside from the findings below. [32]  
Cardiac: Grade 3/6 systolic ejection murmur, unchanged from prior. [32]  
Lungs: Mild bibasilar rales; no wheezes or rhonchi; excellent air movement. [32]  
Extremities: Trace bilateral lower extremity edema. [32]  

## RESULTS
No in-visit laboratory or imaging results were reviewed; clinician planned lab testing (lipid panel, CBC with differential, CMP, and urinalysis). [32]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
**1) Hypertension with elevated/fluctuating home readings and associated lightheadedness/headache:** BP control felt to be out of control. [32]  
- Increase lisinopril to 60 mg twice daily. [32]  
- Start hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily. [32]  
- Home BP monitoring twice daily for the next 2 weeks. [32]  
- Follow up in 2 weeks. [32][38]  

**2) Hyperlipidemia:** Continue atorvastatin. [32]  
- Order cholesterol panel, CBC with differential, CMP, and urinalysis (including to assess kidney status). [32]  

**3) Osteoarthritis:** Continue to stay active (including walking/golfing) without overdoing it. [32]  
- Continue Tylenol as needed for mild pain. [32]  

**Counseling:** Maintain hydration (about 6–8 glasses of water/day; increase with heat or higher activity if feeling dehydrated). [33][34]  
**Alcohol:** Advised to limit beer/alcohol to no more than a couple drinks per week given BP fluctuations. [35][36]

## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Donna is a 37-year-old female with a history of hypertension and diabetes who presents with worsening headaches. [0][1]  
She reports headaches have been occurring more frequently lately and have been getting worse. [1]  
She has noticed her blood pressure has been running higher than usual over the past three weeks and she checks it about once weekly. [1]  
Home blood pressure readings included 160/87 and 175/92, and the clinician noted a prior clinic blood pressure of 124/82. [3][4]  
She has been prescribed lisinopril and reports she takes it but sometimes forgets doses, especially on busy weekends. [4][5]  
She has been checking blood sugars because she wondered if glucose was contributing to the headaches, and reports values around 120 and sometimes down to 100. [6][7]  
She denies increased urination. [8][9]  
For headache relief she takes Tylenol, which helps briefly but the headache returns throughout the day. [10][11]  
Headache severity is about 7/10 at onset and improves to about 4–5/10 after Tylenol, though it remains annoying. [12][13][16][17]  
She reports a history of rare migraines (about once a year or every 1–2 years) but states these headaches are different. [21]  
She thought spring allergies might be contributing but denies associated runny nose, cough, or congestion. [22][23]  
She reports mild tenderness when tapping the forehead, mild cheekbone tenderness, and some neck tenderness. [24][25][28][29][30][31]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
The clinician’s exam impression was no frontal sinus tenderness, no maxillary sinus tenderness, and no lymphadenopathy. [32]  

## RESULTS
No laboratory or imaging results were reviewed during this encounter, but the clinician noted that her last recorded clinic blood pressure was 124/82. [4]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
**1) Headache, worsening:** Continue Tylenol as needed for symptom relief. [32]  
**2) Hypertension, uncontrolled with elevated home readings and intermittent medication adherence:** Increase lisinopril from 20 mg once daily to 40 mg once daily to address hypertension and headaches. [32]  
She was instructed to continue checking her blood pressure. [34]  
**3) Diabetes mellitus:** Order blood work including hemoglobin A1c and other routine labs. [34]  
Provide diabetic education materials for her to review. [34]  
**4) Follow-up:** Patient to come in to be seen by the clinician or Dr. Ruth after labs to review results and ensure blood pressure control. [32][34]  
**5) Activity:** Patient may continue walking/exercise. [35][36]

## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Michael presents after his PCP was concerned about his symptoms and ordered a CT scan. [0]  
He reports intermittent left-sided back pain that varies in character (sometimes sharp) and sometimes moves. [3][5]

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Throat exam: no erythema or swelling, and tonsils are midline. [44]  
Neck: no cervical lymphadenopathy. [44]  
Lungs: clear to auscultation without wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. [44]

Abdomen: no pain with palpation and no rebound or guarding. [46][47][48]  

## RESULTS
CT scan ordered by the PCP reportedly shows findings concerning for possible glomerulonephritis, potentially related to recurrent strep episodes, with “swelling” noted by the clinician. [0][48]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
**1) Suspected glomerulonephritis (possible post-strep association based on CT):** Clinician discussed concern that this could lead to kidney disease/chronic kidney disease and plans further confirmation. [48]  
**Plan:** Order labs including lipid panel, urinalysis, CMP, CBC, vitamin D, and TSH. [48]  

**2) Recurrent strep/sore throats:** No current exam findings of active strep infection today. [44]

## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Billy is a 74-year-old male who presents with cough. [0][58]  
Cough began about 1 week ago and has been getting worse over the last 2 days. [1][2][3]  
He reports productive cough with yellow sputum and has felt “not the greatest” the last two days. [1]  
He has felt warm but has not measured his temperature. [7]  
He denies chills and reports being a little achy at times. [8][9]  
He denies shortness of breath. [10][11]  
He reports nasal congestion with drainage down his throat and mild sinus pressure. [12][13]  
He denies sick contacts at home and works as a teacher. [14][15][16][17]  
He reports a history of hypertension, does not check his blood pressure at home, and did not buy a BP cuff. [18][19][21]  
He believes he is taking lisinopril 20 mg daily (described as a white pill) but is not really watching salt intake. [22][23][25][26][27]  
He also reports right knee injury today after coughing and falling down 4–5 stairs, with pain mostly on the inside of the knee. [31][33][35][37][41]  
He is able to walk and bend/straighten the knee but has a slight limp and no sensation of the knee giving out. [38][41][42][43][45][46][47]  
He has not taken anything specifically for knee pain and has been using OTC cough/cold medication (e.g., DayQuil), which helps the cough a little. [48][49][52][53]  
Regarding diabetes, he is not watching sugar intake and does not check blood sugars. [54][55][56][57]  
He reports taking metformin and believes he takes a medication twice daily with help from his wife organizing his pill box. [59][61]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Vital signs in office: T 98.4°F, BP ~125/62, HR in the 80s, SpO₂ 97% on room air. [66]  
Face: tenderness to palpation over bilateral maxillary sinuses. [67][69][70]  
Nose: bilateral turbinate erythema/edema with yellow discharge. [70]  
Throat: uvula midline with no peritonsillar erythema, edema, or exudate. [70]  
Neck: bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy. [70]  
Lungs: coarse rhonchi bilaterally that clear with cough. [70]  
Heart: regular rate and rhythm without murmur. [70]  
Right knee: pain to palpation with decreased flexion/extension and ecchymosis over the right medial knee, without effusion. [70][71][74]  

## RESULTS
No imaging or lab results were resulted/reviewed during the visit. [74][76][78]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
**1) Cough / viral syndrome with probable mild bronchitis**: Symptoms x1 week, worse x2 days, productive yellow sputum, with exam showing coarse rhonchi that clear with cough; clinician felt this is viral and does not require antibiotics at this time. [1][2][70][74]  
Plan: Supportive care and continue OTC medications; start guaifenesin 600 mg twice daily to help expectorate phlegm. [74]  
Return precautions: If symptoms worsen, patient to call; consider chest X-ray to evaluate for pneumonia if worsening. [74]  

**2) Right knee pain after fall—suspected right medial collateral ligament (MCL) strain**: Fell down 4–5 stairs today while coughing with medial right knee pain and limp; exam with medial ecchymosis and decreased ROM without effusion. [33][35][41][45][74][76]  
Plan: Start meloxicam 15 mg once daily; refer to physical therapy; order right knee X-ray to rule out bony injury. [76]  

**3) Hypertension**: History of hypertension; patient not monitoring BP at home but office BP today was ~125/62. [18][21][66]  
Plan: Continue lisinopril 20 mg daily and order lipid panel. [78]  

**4) Diabetes mellitus**: Patient not monitoring sugar intake or home blood sugars; reports taking metformin (intended dose discussed as 1000 mg twice daily). [55][60][61]  
Plan: Order hemoglobin A1c to assess control and determine if metformin adjustments are needed; counsel to monitor sugar intake and report home blood sugars via the patient portal. [78][79]

note:
## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Steven presents with chronic low back pain for the past couple of years. [0][3] The pain began after a fall onto his back while playing basketball. [5][7] He rates the pain about 7/10 and describes it as sharp with occasional throbbing. [9][11][13] Symptoms fluctuate in intensity, and the pain radiates down the left leg with intermittent toe numbness. [15][17][19] He reports intermittent weakness, especially when trying to stand up or after standing too long. [23][29] Lying on his side improves symptoms, while standing for a while worsens them. [25][29] He reports one episode of bowel incontinence over the last two years, which he attributes to standing too long and drinking, and states it is not consistent. [31][33] Prior treatments include unspecified injections, physical therapy (only mildly helpful), and an unspecified “white pill” pain medication that he later stated worked. [35][39][41][95] He reports being largely sedentary (sits at home all day watching TV) and has gained about 80 pounds in the past year. [43][47] Family history is notable only for a fourth cousin with scoliosis; he is not aware of spine conditions in close relatives. [49][51] He acknowledges a past history of smoking but denies current smoking. [59][67]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Cardiac exam: regular rate and rhythm; no murmur appreciated. [72] Back exam: no overlying erythema or ecchymosis. [72] There is midline tenderness at the L4–L5 disc space with right-sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness. [72] Pain is elicited with lumbar flexion and (mildly) with extension. [73][75] Supine straight leg raise is positive. [76][78] Neuro exam: decreased sensation to light touch at the right lateral thigh (L4 dermatome); patellar and Achilles reflexes are symmetric. [78]  

## RESULTS
MRI shows disc desiccation and a disc bulge with paracentral disc herniation resulting in moderate right neural foraminal stenosis. [80] The clinician assessed these findings (with symptoms and exam) as consistent with L4–L5 herniated disc with radiculopathy. [82]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
1) **Chronic low back pain with L4–L5 herniated disc and radiculopathy**: Based on symptoms (radiation/paresthesias), exam (positive straight leg raise and sensory changes), and MRI findings. [17][78][80][82] Plan is to proceed with a fluoroscopy-guided spinal corticosteroid injection to target inflammation and nerve irritation. [82][92] Patient is anxious about needles and requested a pill instead; clinician offered a prescription for an anxiolytic to take about 3 hours before the injection appointment. [83][84] Patient to schedule the injection at the front desk; procedure expected to take about an hour, and if effective would be limited to about four times per year. [103][104]  

2) **Pain control strategy**: Clinician advised that additional pain pills may not be needed after the steroid injection, but if pain persists after about a day post-injection, they will discuss pain medication options. [92][96] Clinician noted that long-term reliance on pain medication is not preferred. [96]  

3) **Escalation if persistent symptoms**: If pain continues despite injection and medication trial, clinician stated surgery may be considered, with intent to try other options first. [96][98]

note:
## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Sharon presents with right shoulder pain. [1][2]  
Pain began about 3 weeks ago while remodeling her house when she was on a ladder painting/reaching out, fell, and braced herself. [2][3][4]  
She iced the arm and took Advil/ibuprofen, which helped somewhat. [5][12][15]  
This is her first episode of right shoulder pain. [6][7]  
She can move the right arm but has pain with lifting/raising the arm overhead. [8][9]  
Pain is present all the time with intermittent worsening. [10][11]  
Pain is 6/10 without medication and improves to 4/10 with medication. [12][13][14][15]  
She had numbness/tingling initially after the injury but this has resolved. [24][25]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Vitals were reported as good. [26]  
Right shoulder exam: limited active and passive range of motion with tenderness over the greater tuberosity of the humerus, and no tenderness at the sternoclavicular joint. [26]  
Neurovascular exam: strong bilateral hand grips, brisk capillary refill (<3 seconds), and intact/equal sensation to touch. [26]  

## RESULTS
Right shoulder x-ray obtained today showed no fracture or bony abnormality. [26]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
Right shoulder pain after fall/trauma, most consistent with rotator cuff tendinopathy. [3][26]  
Order MRI of the right shoulder to evaluate for other pathology. [26]  
Refer to physical therapy for 6–8 weeks and reassess response before considering additional treatments such as a steroid injection. [26]  
Continue an NSAID if tolerated and use Tylenol for pain if she can tolerate it. [26]  
Follow up in 2 weeks to reassess symptoms and adjust the treatment plan as needed. [28][29]  
Patient agrees with the plan and is hoping for symptom relief. [27][28]

note:
## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Matthew presents for evaluation of vision changes, with particular trouble in the right eye. [2]  
He reports difficulty adjusting to dark/dim environments, especially when eating out at night. [3][5]  
Per technician notes reviewed by the clinician, he has decreased vision when reading, especially in dark places. [6]  
He also reports that straight lines (e.g., door frames and the paneling grooves in his office) appear crooked/wavy, which has been present for a while and has worsened over the last 3–4 months. [6][7]  
He currently smokes about 1 pack per day and has had difficulty quitting despite trying hypnosis, lozenges, and chewing gum. [16][17]  
He has a history of high blood pressure and reports he has not taken his blood pressure pill for a while because he has trouble remembering to take it. [18][19]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Eye exam/anterior segment exam: posterior chamber intraocular lenses present in both eyes. [22]  
Right eye: cornea clear; no NVI; cup-to-disc ratio 0.2; retina attached 360° with no holes/tears; RPE changes in the fovea; macular drusen with retinal thickening, subretinal fluid, exudate, and hemorrhage. [22]  
Left eye: cornea clear; cup-to-disc ratio 0.3; RPE changes in the fovea; no hemorrhage or subretinal fluid; retina attached appropriately. [22]  

## RESULTS
OCT shows drusen deposits under the RPE and subretinal fluid, with SRF accumulation and a choroidal neovascular membrane visualized. [22]  
Fluorescein angiography shows a focal area of leakage in the right eye consistent with neovascular age-related macular degeneration. [22]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
**1) Neovascular (wet) age-related macular degeneration, right eye, with subretinal fluid and hemorrhage causing visual distortion/decreased vision.** [22]  
The clinician discussed that this is an advanced stage that can lead to more severe vision loss due to abnormal blood vessels growing beneath the retina that leak fluid and blood, potentially causing a central blind spot. [22]  
Plan is intravitreal Lucentis injections once monthly, with the eye numbed beforehand to minimize pain. [22]  
The clinician counseled that injections over time can slow vision decline and possibly stop it, though some vision loss may still occur. [28]  
Patient’s questions/concerns about pain were addressed, and he agreed to proceed as long as it will not hurt a lot. [23][29]  
Nurse will prep the patient and the first injection will be performed today. [32][33]  

**2) Hypertension with medication nonadherence.** [18][19]  
The clinician advised restarting blood pressure medication as prescribed starting today and suggested placing the pill bottle on the counter to help with adherence. [20]  

**3) Tobacco use (1 PPD).** [16][17]  
The clinician advised cutting back or quitting smoking altogether. [18]

## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Joe presents for evaluation of a right knee injury with swelling and pain. [0][1][11]  
He reports the injury occurred while skiing on Saturday when he lost control in poor visibility, did a partial cartwheel, felt a “pop” in the right knee, and then had immediate knee pain. [3][5][11]  
Since the injury, he has been able to walk a little but notes persistent swelling and pain. [6][7][1]  
He has been treating symptoms with Advil, icing on and off, and occasional heat. [8][9]  
He denies any prior knee injuries. [10][11]  
After the fall, he also noticed his shoulder felt bruised, as he landed on that shoulder, with tenderness when raising the arm. [24][25][27][29]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Vital signs were normal: T 97.9°F, HR 64, RR 16, BP 110/74, SpO₂ 98%. [12]  
Right knee exam showed edema and ecchymosis with pain to palpation. [12][14]  
He had pain with flexion and extension but reported essentially full range of motion, limited by tightness and mild pain. [14][15][16][17]  
Shoulder exam showed bruising, and the patient reported tenderness with raising the arm. [28][29]  

## RESULTS
Right knee x-ray showed no fracture and no bony abnormalities. [18]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
1) **Right knee contusion after skiing injury**: Clinician impression was knee contusion with negative x-ray for fracture/bony abnormality. [3][18]  
Plan: rest and apply ice 2–3 times per day for 20 minutes at a time, and use NSAIDs such as ibuprofen every 6–8 hours as needed. [18]  
If symptoms do not improve, patient to return for reassessment and possible physical therapy. [18]  

2) **Shoulder pain/bruising after fall (concern for injury)**: Patient reports bruising and tenderness with arm elevation, and bruising was seen on exam. [25][28][29]  
Plan: obtain an x-ray of the right shoulder, with nurse to escort patient for imaging, then follow up to review results. [30]  

Patient had no questions and agreed with the plan. [19][31]

note:
## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Angela is here for follow-up after a recent diagnosis of stage III non-small cell lung cancer and to assess how she is doing on neoadjuvant chemotherapy. [2]  
She reports new/worsening shortness of breath over the weekend with markedly decreased exercise tolerance (previously walked her dog around the block/mile-long walks daily, now cannot walk down the street and had her husband walk the dog). [3][5]  
She reports a dry cough without phlegm. [8][9]  
She denies fever. [10][11]  
She reports painful swallowing (odynophagia) that feels more in her chest, without sensation of food getting stuck. [13][15]  
Because of the pain, she has been drinking less over the weekend but is still able to take liquids and has been using Ensure and a milkshake. [17]  
Emotionally, she feels devastated by the diagnosis but reports good support from her husband and daughter. [19]  
She is receiving cisplatin/etoposide chemotherapy (last dose a few days ago) and reports mild nausea and fatigue that improved with prescribed ondansetron and rest. [20][21]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Vitals were normal and pulse oximetry was okay. [22]  
Neck exam: no cervical lymphadenopathy and no subclavicular adenopathy. [22]  
Cardiac exam: regular rate and rhythm, no murmur. [22]  
Lung exam: faint bilateral crackles. [22]  
Skin: erythema on the left anterior chest, felt possibly consistent with radiation-related skin changes/burn. [22]  
Extremities: no lower extremity edema. [22]  

## RESULTS
Chest X-ray obtained prior to the visit showed mild radiation pneumonitis (lung inflammation due to radiation). [22]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
**1) Stage III non-small cell lung cancer with 4.4 cm left upper lobe nodule, on neoadjuvant therapy.** [2][4]  
- Continue current chemotherapy regimen and continue current radiation dosing (noted as 45 “grade”). [22]  
- After completion of the regimen, plan additional testing and consider surgical resection if the tumor has decreased enough for removal. [22]  

**2) Mild radiation pneumonitis with shortness of breath.** [22][3]  
- Start prednisone 40 mg PO daily x 5 days to reduce lung inflammation and help breathing so radiation can continue. [22]  
- Reviewed that short course is less likely to cause significant weight gain/jitteriness; may have some muscle aches when stopping after 5 days. [23][24]  

**3) Odynophagia likely related to radiation-associated inflammation.** [13][22]  
- Prescribe lidocaine “swish and swallow” up to 4 times/day; patient may eat immediately afterward to facilitate oral intake. [24]  
- Encourage maintaining hydration and nutrition during chemotherapy/radiation (continue liquids as tolerated). [17][18][24]  

**Follow-up:** Return visit in 2 weeks. [28]

## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Joshua presents with 2 weeks of intermittent right flank pain radiating to the groin, with occasional bouts of worse pain. [3][5][7]  
Pain varies with how much he has been drinking and with activity, and he reports a typical severity of 1–2/10 with flares up to 4/10. [7][15]  
He denies dysuria and hematuria. [8][9][10][11]  
He reports one episode of pain radiating to a testicle that later improved. [11]  
He has been taking ibuprofen, which helps somewhat but the pain persists, and he requests stronger medication for the upcoming weekend. [13][37]  
He denies nausea and vomiting, and reports possible chills but no fevers. [20][21]  
He reports a strong family history of kidney stones. [22][23][25]  
He has a personal history of kidney stones, previously passing some on his own (typically within a week) and requiring surgery a couple of times. [26][27][29]  
He saw his PCP prior to this visit and was given a urine strainer, which he has been using without passing a stone yet. [38][39]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Blood pressure was noted to be a little elevated, felt likely due to pain, and otherwise vitals/appearance were described as fine. [32]  
Abdomen was non-tender to palpation. [32][33][36]  
Right costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness was present. [34][35][36]  

## RESULTS
CT scan obtained prior to the visit showed a 0.5 cm stone in the proximal right ureter. [36]  
CT showed no evidence of hydronephrosis/obstruction. [36]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
**Right proximal ureteral stone (0.5 cm) with renal colic symptoms; no hydronephrosis on CT.** [36]  
Plan for conservative management with increased oral fluids to help pass the stone. [36]  
Continue ibuprofen for pain control, and add tramadol 50 mg for stronger pain control as needed; ibuprofen may be used for breakthrough pain. [36][38]  
Continue to use the urine strainer with each urination to try to catch the stone. [40][39]  
Order BMP, urinalysis, and urine culture; antibiotics will be considered depending on results though clinician felt infection was unlikely. [40]  
Follow up in 1–2 weeks (planned in ~2 weeks), and if still symptomatic/stone persists, discuss further treatment options including possible lithotripsy. [40][44]

note:
## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Frank presents with shortness of breath for about 1 week and reports he is “not great.” [0][1][3]  
He had a similar episode about 6 months ago that improved after his Lasix dose was changed. [5]  
He has a history of coronary artery disease with MI in 2018 s/p stent to the right coronary artery and a prior reported ejection fraction of ~35%. [6]  
He reports recent dietary indiscretion over the holidays despite generally trying to watch salt intake. [6][7]  
He endorses definite leg swelling. [8][9]  
He is taking Lasix 40 mg once daily as prescribed and denies decreased urine output, though he notes nocturia 1–2 times nightly which is more than before. [10][11][12][13][15]  
Dyspnea is mainly exertional (walking around, stairs, walking quickly) rather than at rest. [16][17]  
He reports worsening orthopnea, now needing two pillows. [18][19]  
He denies current paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, though it occurred during the episode 6 months ago. [20][21]  
He last saw his nephrologist about 1 month ago and has known stage 3 kidney dysfunction. [22][23][24]  
Despite being advised to avoid NSAIDs, he took Motrin for a recent episode of foot pain about a week ago and stopped as soon as he could; he feels Tylenol is not very effective. [24][25][26][27]  
He has not previously received counseling on potassium restriction and asked what foods are high in potassium. [30][31][33]  
He reports a recent episode of foot pain lasting 4–5 days that slowly resolved, located just in front of the big toe on one foot, with no prior history of gout diagnosis. [51][53][55][57][58][59]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Vital signs in office: BP ~135/80. [46]  
Neck: jugular venous distention to ~9 cm; no carotid bruits. [46]  
Lungs: bibasilar crackles with occasional rhonchi that clear with cough. [46]  
Heart: 3/6 systolic ejection murmur at the left base. [46]  
Extremities: 1–2+ pitting edema bilaterally. [46]  

## RESULTS
No diagnostic test results were reviewed during the visit; clinician ordered chest X-ray, echocardiogram, comprehensive metabolic panel, and uric acid level. [50][60]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
**1) Shortness of breath due to acute heart failure exacerbation with volume overload.** [46]  
Increase Lasix from 40 mg daily to 80 mg daily, start by taking the increased dose in the morning, and consider adding a second afternoon dose (~4 PM) if needed (avoid late dosing). [46][48]  
Patient to call in 2 days with weight update after increasing diuretic, and call sooner for worsening shortness of breath (may require hospital admission for IV diuretics). [46]  
Order chest X-ray to ensure no other process is being missed. [50]  
Counseled to watch salt intake; clinician suspects dietary indiscretion may have contributed and will place dietitian referral. [64][38]  

**2) Coronary artery disease with prior MI (2018) s/p RCA stent; systolic murmur.** [6][46]  
Order repeat echocardiogram to evaluate murmur and reassess cardiac function. [50]  
Clinician will call Dr. White to arrange cardiology evaluation. [50]  

**3) Stage 3 chronic kidney disease.** [24]  
Avoid NSAIDs (Motrin/Advil) and use Tylenol; if pain remains significant, patient to call to discuss alternative medications/therapies. [50][28]  
Order comprehensive metabolic panel to assess kidney function stability. [50]  
Provided initial counseling on potassium restriction (avoid high-potassium foods such as bananas, oranges, potatoes) and will refer to dietitian for more detailed guidance. [34][38]  

**4) Possible gout flare (recent podagra-like pain).** [57][60]  
Order uric acid level and discuss kidney-safe alternative medication options if future flares occur. [60]
